speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Romek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Yeah, you're going to-- I'm going to ask you to tell me your name at birth, and you'll spell it, too. I don't even have all my sheets. But I think I should have this down pat, by now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Isabel Alcoff. I'll be conducting an interview with Robert Mendler. The date is November 17, 1996, and we're in Latrobe, Pennsylvania. The interview will be conducted in English. Can you give me your birth name, date of birth, and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Romek Reibeisen-- R-E-I-B-E-I-S-E-N. I was born in a small town in Poland, Nowy-Targ-- capital N-O-W-Y, dash, capital T-A-R-G. It's not too far from-- about 60 kilometers from Krakow, a large city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 6th, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents name-- my father's name was Mundek Mendel Reibeisen-- R-E-I-B-E-I-S-E-N. My mother's name, Hermina-- H-E-R-M-I-N-A, Mendler Reibeisen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, where were your parents from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in Bobowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not too far from Nowy Sacz-- which in Yiddish they call Neu-Sandez.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was born in a small village, Harklowa. It's not too far from Nowy-Targ.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they happen to come to Nowy-Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's been for generation-- hundreds of years, maybe. Our family lived in-- my grandfather, I know, he-- he came from the small village, then moved to Nowy-Targ. And they were in business, my grandfather. The First World War, he had a beer garden-- sell whiskey, things like that. Later on, he opened up a soda water factory. We used to manufacture seltzer water, Pepsi Colas, like soft drinks. This was my grandfather's business.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your maternal grandfather, or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My maternal grandfather. This was my mother's father, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your mother's family lived in the area. And how about your father's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family, they lived in Neu-Sandez, Nowy Sacz. They were about birth-- about [NON-ENGLISH]. My grandfather was a Hasid, a Bobowa Hasid. And they had a large family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father had two other brothers, besides him. There were three brothers. And there were-- oh, my. Let's see, there were-- one, two, three, four, five, six sisters. Large family. Of course, they all were married, their children, and so far down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's family, did she come from a large family also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's family also come from large family. There were four brothers. There were three sisters, and two passed away. You know, in Europe, people have large families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother stay at home when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When my father married my mother-- so my father moved from Neu-Sandez to Nowy-Targ, and to stay with my mother's parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My home in Nowy-Targ-- Nowy-Targ was a small town. It had no industries, merely several workshop. But there were many Jewish artisans and craftsmen of considerable skill and enterprise. The main synagogue in our town was built in modern style. It accommodate 800 worshipers. The first cantor for the main synagogue was the very famous Yossele Rosenblatt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After a while, he was engaged as the chief cantor in the Hamburg temple in Germany, and later went to the United States, where he became worldwide known. Nowy-Targ also had several other synagogues, some of Hasidic centers of learning, which added greatly to the Hasidic atmosphere in town. The Jewish cemetery in Nowy-Targ was outside the town, at one time. It abounded with headstone for the graves-- of the graves of the great rabbis and community leaders, as well as simple folks. During the Holocaust, the cemetery witnessed a tragic end of Nowy-Targ Jewery. In August 30, 1942, following the last round up, the town there remains several hundred Jews. 500 of them were taken there, and tortured, and shot. Among them was my father. Their bodies were thrown into the common grave. Today, this cemetery is neglected, forgotten, markers of the past. The Jewish population in Nowy-Targ were 3,000, before the war. 39 of us remain alive today, after the war. We had, also, one of the largest Zionist movement organizations in the country. To name a few, Akiva, Mizrahi, [NON-ENGLISH], Betar. We also-- we also were very active in sports competition against our Polish friends and enemies. I remember very well, when I was growing up, the year 1938, anti-Semitism in our town became very strong and active. Polish anti-Semitism became organized-- the Endeks, we used to call them, the Endeks. What we used to call them, strung banners with anti-Semitic slogans across the streets. They also set up picket line in front of the Jewish stores, to prevent Poles from entering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a-- we had a had a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big a house was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five rooms. In front of our house we had a store. My uncle had a business there, grocery store. Back of the house was soda water factory-- that's where we did soda water, like I said before. And my father, he was a candy maker. We had factory-- he had a candy factory. So he manufactured hard candy, chocolate candy, all the sweets good. We-- we employed quite a few people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one brother, older-- three years older than myself. And I had a younger sister. She was three years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Gienek, in Yiddish, Gershon. He was three years older than me. My sister, her name was Henia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was she born? She was born in April, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was a little younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was three years younger than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your school life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was growing up, when I was three years old I started to go to cheder. This was a must. My father came-- to give a little bit of background-- my father came from very religious family, like I mentioned before. He was a Hasid, Babowa Hasid. My grandfather was very orthodox Jew, from my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in my home, we keep strictly kosher. There was no such a thing. Friday night, Friday Shabbat-- business was closed at noon, Friday. We all went-- father took my brother and myself to the mikvah, which is a ritual bath for the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: After mikvah, we went home, got ready for shul. Went to shul Friday night, come home. Meantime, mother light the candles of the Shabbat. Business was closed, no business. We observe very strict, in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what type of foods did she prepare for you on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Shabbat, every Friday was cholent, because we weren't allowed to cook at home. We did have two Gentile girls working for us. So my mother prepared the cholent. She made her own challah, and all the trimmings. We used to send the cholent to the baker. The oven was still hot from the bake-- when the bake was previously was done. So we kept them down there. And Shabbat morning, we came from shul, the girl went, pick it up, brought home. To begin with, Friday night, I remember we had a-- the first course was gefilte fish. There was chicken soup, chicken noodle soup, and chicken, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and all that goes with it. She was an excellent cook, my mother-- very good cook, baker and cook.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, at dinner?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like pulke-- you know what pulke is? From chicken, drumstick. This was my favorite. I still like it today, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were two Gentile housekeepers or maids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, maids-- two maids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived with us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did anybody else live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had, also, my father uncle. He was a bachelor, and was older man. He lived with us. Then we had another boy, which we adopt. So there were three, five of us-- six, seven, eight, nine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the boy that you adopted?

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents-- there were three children in the family. The parents got into fight-- every Thursday, we had market in our hometown. The parents got into fight with each other, and they abandoned the children. They left the children. There was a-- the older boy, he was then around eight years old. There was a sister. She must've been about six. And the younger boy, five years old. And the children start to cry. The mother went one way, the father went the other way. They abandoned the children. And my mother took the younger one to our house, and we raise him until the end-- until the final solution. The older boy, he went to orphanage. He lives in Israel. Every time I go to Israel, we have a chance to-- we see each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Olek Feler. He's got Israeli name, different name, today. And his sister, a Gentile people took her. Actually, they stole her from the market, and they took her with them. She survived the war, ans she was brought up as a Christian. And she married-- today, she is married. And the oldest boy, he survive. So he goes to Poland almost every other year, to see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the younger boy, who lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young one was killed. He was killed. When we were-- the final solution was in our town, he ran away from home. He didn't want to go with us. He ran away. And he was hiding. Our Gentile friends, they hid him. And our chief from Gestapo, he saw him. And he warn him, a few times-- you better disappear. If not, I'm going to have to kill you. Finally, came to a point, he caught him, and he shot him. Killed him right by the river in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now your sister was with your mother, you said, on Shabbat. Tell me what type of-- what was the difference between the way she was raised and the way the boys were raised?

speaker: interviewee
text: What different was between us-- when I started-- when I was three years old, I started to go to cheder. This was a must. If I went to public school-- I start to go to public school when I was seven years old. This the way in Europe. But meantime, I had to go to cheder-- they learned us all the things, what had to be done. We had a very good teacher. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. He was very strict with us. Many, many times used to get a licking from him, and from my father too, once in awhile-- you know, if you didn't do a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: After cheder, I came home. So my father started to question us, all kind of questions, when he came home from work-- you know, what did you learn? It was very-- my father was very, very strict.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I don't like it. I'll be honest with you, I used to hate to go to cheder. But I'm very grateful, today, for that. At least I can sit down with some people that know a little bit about Bible, or [NON-ENGLISH], I can discuss this thing-- which I'm very grateful I learned that. And my sister was always with my mother. She was just a little girl, you know. And I remember, my mother on Friday used to make the challah, twist challah, you know. She did about six or seven twists. And my sister, she give a piece of dough, and she used to teach her how to do that, too. She was just a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she allowed to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister just started to go to school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have visits from your family, from out of town, or when holidays came, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very close family. Don't forget, in our town there was my mother's sister-- two sister, they live in our town, also, and their family. So Saturday, after lunch they all came to our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what do you do? You entertain yourself. My aunt, once-- one of my mother's sister, my aunt, she play mandolin. Her husband play fiddle. So they play, and we all sang.

speaker: interviewee
text: Myself, my kids-- like myself, my brother, we were a little bit older, then-- we went to the Zionist organization. And we learn Hebrew-- not Hebrew, Ivrit. It was very happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that on Saturdays?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on Saturday, yeah. Because during the week, we can't go to the Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Describe the Zionist organization, what activities you did there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess it was several different organization. And one tried to compete with each other, too-- like for instance, Betar was a difference, right-wing form of Zionist. And the other were different. And I started, I remember, the Zionist organization was Akiba. After a while, when I was growing up, when I was around eight, nine years old, 10 years old, I needed-- I needed more action. And that's when I started to go to Betar. Betar was more stronger organization. They learn how to-- we were-- we were always taught, someday we have to liberate our country with force, not with money. Again, like Akiba, [NON-ENGLISH] who spend the money, this to buy part of the country in Palestine, then. And our leader was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a leader from Betar. I remember, one time, you know, he came to our hometown and gave speech-- because he used to live in Krakow. It's not too far. We saw the brown shirt, dark brown with the blue handkerchiefs, here. And everybody used to carry a stick-- this was our weapon. Because many times we got into little problem with the Polish scouts, Boy Scouts. We used to fought with them. They used to attack us. We used to protect ourselves with them. Because we live-- my town, where I live, was right in the mountains. This was most beautiful part in Poland-- Nowy-Targ, Zakopane, right in the Carpathian mountain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Shabbos afternoon, Shabbat, you know, we used to go, always, hiking in the mountains. So we got into a lot of problems, sometimes. They used to throw stones at us, used to attack us. So we used to learn how to protect ourselves. Someday, we always dream-- our dream was to go to Palestine. This day was no Israel. Did your family ever discuss a real plan to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. They were very content like it was, in Poland. Because, you know, life-- we were-- we had a happy life. But beside that, still I remember-- like at nights, we had to put the shutters on our window. We had three dogs running loose in our backyard, somebody shouldn't break into our, for the factory and do damage. They used to break the windows in Jewish homes-- the Endeks, anti-Semite. And there was a lot of problem with it, too, in the school. We weren't allowed to, much, associate with Gentile friends, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What type of problems did you have in school? Was there any problem with registering for school, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was, actually, I didn't realize that, because it's been like this for generations. For instance, they used to taught the religion in school. We had to get out of the classes. And we always have problem. They used to call us [NON-ENGLISH], all kind of names. They used to beat us up. Some friends of mine used to wear payots. They had to hid the payots behind the ears. It was a lot of problem. But myself, you know, I had my own gang. I was the leader. I was always the leader. My friends, they can vouch for that. Even today, still, when I-- of course, we taught-- we always learned religion at school, too-- afternoon, after the session. For instance, went to school at 8 o'clock in the morning. We were in school until 1 o'clock afternoon. Then you went home for lunch, back to school. This was twice a week, religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a professor who used to teach us Jewish religion. His name was Pressar. And everybody knows-- and everybody knew me, about Romek. And I had like my own gang.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always the first one. He called me up, general, [NON-ENGLISH]. Come over here, general. He was very tough. He asked me question. If I don't know the question, he used to pull my ears. He used to like me a lot, but he used to pull my ears, to pull my hair. If he knew if I don't know the question, nobody else knew from my gang. And we had a bunch of kids, you know, and I was like I was the leader. Even today, friends of mine-- which, not too many was left-- they still call me general-- my friends in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your friends do, when you weren't in school or cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have very much time, because after school, public school, we had to go to cheder. So I went to cheder. Around 3 o'clock afternoon, and I didn't get home until 8 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes we did. This was on the weekends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What type of things did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to play soccer. Here, we call football. Here, they call soccer. We used to play [NON-ENGLISH], it's like a baseball. We used to run, 100 meters. You know, we were very athletic, there. I was very athletic type person, myself. I always love sport. Summertime--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any tournaments, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yeah. We used to compete against our Gentile friends. I also belonged to Akiba, no, to Maccabee. This was a sports club. I was a champion at the ping pong table, you know. So we learned different things, also-- how to ski, because ski you must. Because wintertime, we used to get early snow. When you start to walk, you start to ski, because that's the only way you can move around in our town. We live in the mountain, don't forget. We used to get early snow in November. Stay till-- the snow stayed until May. So you want to go to school, or you want to go to cheder, or something, you have to go on a ski, because the snow was so deep. There was no snow plowers, nothing like that. Snow was beautiful. We couldn't wait to get the snow. There was no pollution, always white. Snow was beautiful. In the weekends, for instance, like wintertime, we had horses at home, because we had-- there was no cars, then, no trucks. And we needed transportation for our business. My father needed to deliver merchandise to the stores. We had a soda water factory. We had a candy factory. We had to deliver to the stores, to the country, to the stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had six horses at home, one time. Then we had four horses. In wintertime, Sunday for instance, my father took off, took his family. My mother, we had a big sled, and horses. And me and my brother, we were behind the sled on a rope, on the ski. And he was driving the horse. Did you ever go away from Nowy-Targ, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go, every year for a few days, to see my grandfather in Neu-Sandez, Nowy Sacz, and my grandmother. My father's family, with the sisters and brothers, all live in Nowy Sacz. So I went down and spend a couple weeks with them. My grandfather, he was always anxious for me to come over there. As a matter of fact, when I was bar mitzvahed, I-- I had to go to my grandfather's hometown, Neu-Sandez, because he was so religious in--

speaker: interviewer
text: He wouldn't travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wouldn't, you know. And he wouldn't travel, so I had to go down there-- had a bar mitzvah in his hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was a bar mitzvah like, in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was bar mitzvahed, I was dressed up like a regular Hasid, the [NON-ENGLISH] and a girdle. And I had to-- everything was in Yiddish. Everything's in Hebrew. I didn't conduct the whole services, only when they call you for the Torah. Of course, I knew that very well, because I had a good training.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we spoke Polish, German, and Yiddish-- mostly, we Polish and German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, Polish. In the cheder, again, we learned Polish, and German, and Yiddish. For instance, when we study [NON-ENGLISH], so we have to translate every syllable, every word, every pentola, you have to translate, which mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that your mother had two sisters in town. Can you tell me what their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my mother older sister name was Regina. She had two daughters-- she was married, of course. Her husband, he was dealing with the-- money. He used to trade money, dollars for Polish money, and so for on. He used to deal-- they say in Polish [POLISH]. They had two daughters. The older daughter's name was Anda. She was a professor at the university, in the Jagiellonian University in Krakow. Her sister, the younger sister, her name was Frydka. And she was a dress maker-- very, very famous dress maker.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your mother's other sisters name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's other sister was Mala. And her husband's name was Gustav. Their two children-- Anna, she was the same age as my sister. And then their son-- the younger son-- Leon. He was three years younger than, again, than his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did Mala do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a housewife. Her husband has a grocery store in our house, in the front. It was a small store, sell groceries and things like that.
